metadata.summary: Index and selection guide for 32 UX methodologies covering heuristics, research, design, and advanced topics. Agent role: select, scaffold, and synthesize.

content.title: UX/UI Methodologies — Complete Index
content.summary: Index and selection guide for 32 core UX methodologies covering Nielsen's 10 usability heuristics, 6 research methods (interviews, usability testing, surveys, card sorting, personas, journey mapping), 9 design methods (wireframing, prototyping, A/B testing, heuristic evaluation, cognitive walkthrough, and others), and 7 advanced topics (IA framework, IA templates, mobile UX, voice UI, diary studies, competitive analysis, content audit). Agent role: select, scaffold, and synthesize — not replace human participant work.

why.p: Applying the wrong UX method wastes research budget and produces misleading artifacts. Method selection depends on the question type (qualitative vs. quantitative, generative vs. evaluative), user access level, and timeline. This index maps problem types to methods so agents route correctly instead of defaulting to the most familiar technique.

list.item: Agent must select an appropriate UX research or design method for a given product problem
Automated heuristic evaluation against Nielsen's 10 usability principles is needed
Sprint requires a structured UX deliverable (persona, journey map, IA sitemap) that an agent can scaffold
UX review of an existing design must be documented systematically before a critique session
Cognitive walkthrough must be scripted for a new user flow
User interviews, usability testing, focus groups, diary studies — require real human participants; agent cannot substitute for moderation or empathy-based interpretation
Card sorting, tree testing — require actual user responses; agent can analyze results but not generate them
A/B testing — requires live traffic; agent can design the test and analyze results, not run it
Any method where output quality depends on observed human behavior in context

section.name: method-index
section.title: UX Method Index — 32 Methodologies
section.p: Complete index of 32 UX/UI methodologies mapped to problem type, artifact produced, and required inputs.

list.item: visibility-of-system-status — problem: users unaware of system state; artifact: feedback design spec; input: UI description or prototype
match-real-world — problem: unfamiliar language/conventions; artifact: language and metaphor review; input: UI copy and interaction patterns
user-control-freedom — problem: users trapped in flows without exits; artifact: undo/redo and exit design; input: user flow diagrams
consistency-standards — problem: inconsistent UI behavior; artifact: consistency audit; input: component library and flow screenshots
error-prevention — problem: users make preventable errors; artifact: confirmation and constraint design; input: high-risk action list
recognition-over-recall — problem: users must memorize UI state; artifact: visibility and affordance recommendations; input: task list
flexibility-efficiency — problem: no accelerators for expert users; artifact: shortcut and personalization spec; input: user segmentation data
aesthetic-minimalist — problem: irrelevant information clutters UI; artifact: content priority matrix; input: current UI screens
error-recovery — problem: users cannot recover from errors; artifact: error message copy and recovery flow; input: error taxonomy
help-documentation — problem: users cannot find help; artifact: help architecture and task-focused docs; input: support ticket analysis
user-interviews — question: why? (qualitative motivations and mental models); artifact: interview synthesis, affinity clusters; input: recruited participants, interview guide
usability-testing — question: can users complete tasks? (observable behavior); artifact: usability findings report; input: prototype + recruited participants
surveys — question: how many? (scale, frequency, satisfaction); artifact: quantitative dataset, SUS score; input: survey distribution channel
card-sorting — question: how do users mentally organize content?; artifact: IA recommendations; input: content list + recruited participants
personas — question: who are our users?; artifact: persona canvases; input: actual user interview data or survey responses
journey-mapping — question: what is the end-to-end user experience?; artifact: journey map with emotions and pain points; input: research data or explicit hypothesis label
wireframing — question: what is the layout and content hierarchy?; artifact: low-fidelity wireframes; input: requirements or user stories
prototyping — question: does this interaction work?; artifact: interactive prototype; input: wireframes or design direction
ab-testing — question: which version performs better?; artifact: statistical significance report; input: live traffic, two variants
heuristic-evaluation — question: does this UI violate usability principles?; artifact: heuristic scorecard; input: UI access (screenshots, prototype, or live product)
contextual-inquiry — question: how do users work in their natural environment?; artifact: observation notes, workflow diagrams; input: access to users in their work context
focus-groups — question: what are group attitudes and reactions?; artifact: synthesis report; input: 6-10 recruited participants
tree-testing — question: can users find things in the navigation structure?; artifact: directness score, success rate per task; input: navigation tree + recruited participants
a11y-basics — question: are basic accessibility fundamentals in place?; artifact: quick checklist results; input: UI access
a11y-testing — question: does the product meet WCAG 2.2 AA?; artifact: audit report; input: running product + AT tools
design-critique — question: what can be improved in this design?; artifact: critique notes and action items; input: design artifact + structured critique framework
competitive-analysis — question: what are competitors doing and where are the gaps?; artifact: comparison matrix; input: 3+ direct competitors + 2 adjacent products
content-audit — question: what content exists and what is its quality?; artifact: content inventory spreadsheet; input: live site or content management system access
cognitive-walkthrough — question: can a new user learn this interface without help?; artifact: learnability findings; input: user flow + task list
ia-framework — question: how should content and navigation be structured?; artifact: IA framework document; input: content inventory
ia-templates — question: which IA patterns apply here?; artifact: IA templates and sitemap; input: content types and user goals
mobile-ux-basics — question: does this work on mobile?; artifact: mobile design recommendations; input: mobile design files or prototypes
mobile-ux-patterns — question: which mobile patterns are appropriate?; artifact: pattern selection and specifications; input: use case list
voice-ui-basics — question: how should a voice interface be structured?; artifact: VUI framework and dialogue scripts; input: use case and platform target
voice-ui-patterns — question: which voice interaction patterns apply?; artifact: prompt writing guide and error recovery scripts; input: VUI platform guidelines
diary-study-basics — question: how do user behaviors change over time?; artifact: study plan and entry protocol; input: research question + participant pool
diary-study-execution — question: what longitudinal patterns emerged?; artifact: temporal pattern analysis; input: diary entries over time

rule.statement: Agents can scaffold, template, and synthesize UX deliverables. Agents cannot recruit, moderate, or interpret participant behavior for user interviews, usability testing, card sorting, tree testing, diary studies, focus groups, or A/B testing.
rule.rationale: These methods depend on observed human behavior in context. An agent generating synthetic "participant responses" produces stereotypes and invalid research artifacts.

rule.statement: When an agent generates a persona without real user data as input, it must label the artifact explicitly: "SYNTHETIC PERSONA — not grounded in user research. Treat as a hypothesis only."
rule.rationale: Synthetic personas look identical to research-grounded personas but encode the designer's assumptions, not actual user behavior. Mislabeling leads to design decisions based on fiction.

rule.statement: Journey maps generated by an agent without real user research data must be labeled: "HYPOTHESIS MAP — assumed, not validated." Every emotional state and pain point is an assumption until validated with real users.
rule.rationale: Journey maps without the label appear authoritative. Teams downstream act on them as validated research, encoding assumptions into product decisions.

rule.statement: When running a heuristic evaluation, an agent must have access to the actual UI (screenshot, DOM export, or prototype URL) — not just a description. Evaluations from descriptions are evaluations of descriptions, not of the interface.
rule.rationale: LLM heuristic evaluations without seeing the actual UI produce plausible-sounding but shallow findings. The agent must state this limitation explicitly if UI access is unavailable.

rule.statement: For each heuristic, the agent must output: rating (pass / partial / fail), specific issue citation if partial or fail, severity (0 cosmetic / 1 minor / 2 major / 3 catastrophic), and a proposed fix. Skip heuristics that cannot be evaluated — do not speculate.
rule.rationale: Incomplete heuristic evaluations with missing severity ratings are not actionable. Developers need prioritized fixes, not a list of "this might be an issue."

rule.statement: Match method to question type: use surveys for "how many?", user interviews for "why?", usability testing for "can users do X?", and heuristic evaluation for "does this violate principles?" Do not use an interview to answer a quantitative question or a survey to understand motivation.
rule.rationale: Method-question mismatch produces invalid results. Surveys cannot reveal why; interviews cannot produce statistically significant counts.

antipattern.description: Agent generates persona canvases from demographic assumptions ("Sarah, 32, tech-savvy") without user research data
antipattern.reason: Marketing stereotypes masquerade as user research. Always pass interview quotes or survey data as input, and label synthetic output explicitly.

antipattern.description: Agent drafts survey questions that are leading ("How much do you love our new feature?")
antipattern.reason: LLMs default to positive framing. Prompt explicitly for neutral, balanced question phrasing. Have questions reviewed by someone unfamiliar with the product.

antipattern.description: Agent chunks interview transcripts at arbitrary boundaries when clustering themes
antipattern.reason: Themes at chunk boundaries get lost in affinity clustering. Transcripts over 4000 tokens require careful chunking at speaker-turn boundaries, not character counts.

antipattern.description: Agent generates heuristic evaluations without seeing the actual UI
antipattern.reason: LLM evaluations from text descriptions are plausible-sounding but shallow. Agents must state this limitation explicitly if UI access is unavailable.

antipattern.description: Persona generation or journey maps treated as validated research when generated from synthetic data
antipattern.reason: Synthetic artifacts encode designer assumptions, not actual user behavior. Always label clearly and note that these are hypotheses until validated with real users.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.title: Agentic Workflow
section.p: A Claude subagent is most useful as a methodology scaffolding and synthesis engine within this knowledge base: select the right method for a stated problem, generate structured templates (persona canvas, journey map skeleton, IA sitemap draft), and synthesize research findings into design recommendations. The agent drives analytical and documentary tasks; human designers and researchers own all participant-facing activities. Subagent handoffs are appropriate at the point where real user data arrives (after interviews, surveys, card sorts).

list.item: faion-sdd-executor-agent — executes UX deliverable tasks defined in an SDD implementation plan (e.g., "generate persona canvas", "draft IA sitemap")
Custom ux-synthesizer agent — takes raw user interview notes (text), applies affinity clustering, generates design recommendations

section.name: prompt-patterns
section.title: Prompt Patterns
section.prompt:
You are a UX strategist. Given this product problem statement: <problem>{{problem_description}}</problem>
And these constraints: - Budget: {{budget_level}} (low/medium/high) - Timeline: {{timeline}} sprints - Access to users: {{user_access}} (none/survey/interviews/usability)
Recommend the 3 most appropriate UX methods from this list: [user-interviews, usability-testing, surveys, card-sorting, personas, journey-mapping, wireframing, prototyping, ab-testing, heuristic-evaluation, contextual-inquiry, tree-testing, competitive-analysis, cognitive-walkthrough]
For each: why it fits, what artifacts it produces, what input data it requires.

Apply Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics to evaluate this UI description: <ui_description>{{ui_description}}</ui_description>
For each heuristic, rate compliance: pass / partial / fail. For partial or fail: cite the specific issue and suggest a fix. Skip heuristics that cannot be evaluated from the description alone — do not speculate.

list.item: Match method complexity to the question: a survey answers "how many?", an interview answers "why?" — don't over-engineer
Generate persona canvases from actual interview data, not demographic assumptions; label clearly if synthetic
Use cognitive walkthrough for new user flows before prototyping — cheaper to fix at script stage than post-prototype
Journey maps are hypotheses until validated with real users; mark status explicitly (assumed vs. validated)
Competitive analysis requires at least 3 direct competitors and 2 adjacent products to produce useful benchmarks
Tree testing results below 70% directness score indicate IA restructuring is needed — don't optimize labels first
optimal-workshop API — Tree testing and card sorting data collection | optimalworkshop.com/developers
useberry API — Usability testing result export | useberry.com/api
maze API — Remote usability testing metrics | maze.co/api
userzoom API — Enterprise usability + survey platform | userzoom.com/api
Optimal Workshop (SaaS, Yes — REST API): Card sorting + tree testing; agent can analyze result exports
Maze (SaaS, Yes — REST API): Remote usability testing; agent can generate test scripts and parse metrics
Lookback (SaaS, Partial): Session recording; no API for analysis automation
UserTesting (SaaS, Partial — limited API): Participant recruitment + recordings; analysis requires human review
Dovetail (SaaS, Yes — REST API): Research repository; agent can tag and synthesize notes
Notion / Confluence (SaaS, Yes — REST API): UX deliverable storage; agent can write personas, journey maps as pages
Miro (SaaS, Yes — REST API): Online whiteboard; agent can create affinity diagrams via API

template.purpose: Prints a Nielsen heuristic evaluation scoring template
Agent prompt: recommend 3 UX methods given problem, budget, and user access
Agent prompt: apply Nielsen's 10 heuristics to a UI description

see-also.link: Nielsen, J. "10 Usability Heuristics for User Interface Design"
